source_lists.title: Folk Horror
source_lists.desc:
An ever-expanding collection of folk, horror and folk-horror film and TV (with a dash of dystopian sci-fi, fantasy, paranoid thriller, creepy/magical children's fare and Urban Wyrd), from the British Isles and Ireland (or set therein / made thereby). Cults, curses, demons, hauntology, ghosts, landscape, magic, myth, monsters, madness, music, murderers, paganism, pestilence, phantoms, possession, post-apocalyptica, psychogeography, runes, revenants, rituals, witches and war. 

Inspired by Adam Scovell's book "Folk Horror: Hours Dreadful and Things Strange" and the essay collections published by Folk Horror Revival ("Field Studies" and "Urban Wyrd Volumes I & II"), and extrapolated from there, allowing for the broadest, most inclusive interpretations of the form's many modes. As they say, everything is folk horror.

Recommendations for additions welcome.

source_lists.title: Folk Horror

My list of folk horror films that I watched and want to watch. Currently at 75/175 films.

If you're wondering, what is folk horror? I've written a couple of blog posts about the topic. 

My latest discussion (7/9/23)

My original primer (2/3/21)

Update 6/27/2022: The Museum of Modern Art is doing a 10-week summer horror program, with each week a different theme in horror. One theme is folk horror. Link to the program. Any films from their program that were not already on my list have been added. Films from the program have a link to the program in the notes section. I also made a separate list of the entire program if you are interested, click here.

source_lists.title: Folk Horror
source_lists.desc:
One of my favorite horror sub-genres, folk horror, is usually slower and more atmospheric than typical horror films. Focused more on pagan ideology, witchcraft, nature, or the occult, and usually located in the woods or rural countryside, folk horror brings a distinctly different flavor to such a rich genre. 

If you’re new to folk-horror, The Unholy Trinity (The Blood on Satan’s Claw, The Witchfinder General, The Wicker Man) is the best place to start as they are three of the earliest and best. These films are not always as outright terrifying as other horror movies that are all about the scares, but they have a way of burrowing under your skin with their unassuming, almost pleasant facades—picturesque countrysides, friendly towsfolk, quaint lifestyles—but are always hiding something more sinister underneath.

Here’s a video in a sick May Queen outfit as I talk about my favorite folk horror pieces:

m.youtube.com/watch?v=0o4GpEpu4U0

source_lists.title: Folk Horror
source_lists.desc: Folk Horror is horror that involves folklore, ie my favourite horror of all time. Often verges on the hauntological; often involves localized cryptology, legends, paganism, religious belief, black magic, mythology, forgotten wisdom, human sacrifice, ritual murder, true crime with paranormal lashings. Cults, occultism, obscure knowledge, fate, secret societies whose mythology becomes real; small communities arrayed against authoritarian structures which eventually BECOME authoritarian structures. The past is never just past, even if it's been hidden or forgotten. Often these things are based on/riff off of history, but they don't have to be set in a different time period. Landscape is a very important ingredient. Family and gender issues, bloodline guilt, ecology and politics come baked in.

source_lists.title: Folk Horror
source_lists.desc: "When I proposed doing an introductory class on folk horror a few months ago, I don’t think I fully grasped what I had committed myself to. I’d seen The Unholy Trinity, of course, and a number of other notable films in the sub-genre—The Witch, Midsommar, Children of the Corn, and Kier-La Janisse’s excellent Woodlands Dark and Days Bewitched—but when you do a deep dive into any subject, you find out just how wide and varied it can be. You discover new favorites, reevaluate old ones, and come across some strange oddities that end up living rent-free in your head. The list below is a curated collection of folk horror films that I discussed in my recent class, and a few… more
